title: C&P Exam Survival Guide
desc: What to expect, bring, and say at your Compensation & Pension exam -- and what to do if it goes wrong

sections.heading: :book: What Is a C&P Exam?
sections.body:
A Compensation & Pension exam is VA's way of evaluating your claimed conditions. This is **not a treatment appointment** -- the examiner's sole job is to document your current severity and render a medical opinion on service connection.

> [!important] The exam report goes directly to the VA rater who decides your claim. What the examiner writes is often the single most influential piece of evidence in your entire file.

---

Under `38 CFR 3.159(c)(4)`, VA must provide a medical examination when the record contains:
- Evidence of a **current disability**
- An **in-service event**
- An indication the two **may be associated**

The examiner fills out a **Disability Benefits Questionnaire (DBQ)** -- a standardized form specific to your condition that captures exactly the data VA raters need to assign a rating.

> [!tip] The DBQ is a checklist. The examiner checks boxes and fills in numbers. Understanding what's on YOUR specific DBQ tells you exactly what the examiner will test -- and what you need to be prepared to describe.

> [!warning] Some examiners spend as little as 10-15 minutes with you. That's not much time to capture the full picture of a disability you live with 24/7. Come prepared to communicate efficiently.

sections.heading: :shield: What to Bring
sections.body:
Walk into that exam room locked and loaded with documentation. The examiner may or may not have read your file -- be ready either way.

**Your C&P exam kit:**
- **Government-issued photo ID**
- **Medication list** -- every med, dosage, and frequency
- **Private medical records** not yet submitted to VA
- **Written symptom summary** -- your condition in your own words
- **Buddy statement copies** -- corroboration from people who see your daily struggles
- **Worst-day notes** -- specific examples of your most severe symptoms
- **Condition timeline** -- onset in service through present day

> [!check] The written summary is your secret weapon. Exams are stressful and you WILL forget key details under pressure. Having your symptoms and limitations written down ensures nothing gets missed.

---

**Make your summary specific and functional:**

| Vague (Don't Say) | Specific (Say This) |
|-------------------|--------------------|
| "My back hurts" | "I cannot lift my children" |
| "I have trouble sleeping" | "I wake up 3-4 times per night from pain" |
| "Work is hard" | "I miss 3-4 days of work per month due to flare-ups" |
| "I can't do much" | "I cannot stand for more than 10 minutes" |

> [!tip] Functional impact is what drives ratings. The examiner needs to know what you **cannot do** -- not just what hurts.

sections.heading: :target: How to Describe Your Symptoms
sections.body:
Describe your condition on your **worst days, not your best**. This is the single most important mindset shift for your C&P exam.

> [!danger] Many veterans unconsciously minimize their symptoms because of military culture -- we were trained to push through pain and say "I'm fine." The C&P exam is NOT the place for that. Minimizing here costs you money, period.

---

**The before and after of symptom description:**

| Instead Of | Say |
|-----------|-----|
| "My back hurts sometimes" | "My back pain prevents me from sitting for more than 20 minutes. I have flare-ups 3-4 times a week where the pain is so severe I can't bend to tie my shoes. I wake up 2-3 times a night from the pain. On my worst days, I can barely get out of bed." |

**Be specific about these five dimensions:**
- **Frequency** -- how often symptoms occur
- **Duration** -- how long episodes last
- **Severity** -- pain scale, functional limitations
- **Impact** -- what you can no longer do
- **Flare-ups** -- how often, how severe, how long

> [!check] Practice describing your symptoms out loud before the exam. Use the five dimensions above as your framework. If you can hit all five for each condition, you've given the examiner everything they need.

> [!tip] Bring a spouse, caregiver, or buddy to the exam if possible. They can provide a third-party perspective on how your condition affects daily life -- and they keep you honest about not minimizing.

sections.heading: :search: What Examiners Test for Common Conditions
sections.body:
Each condition has a specific DBQ that drives what the examiner evaluates. Knowing what they're looking for lets you prepare the right information.

---

**PTSD/Mental Health** (`DC 9411`):
- Specific **stressor events** and their details
- Symptom **frequency and severity**
- **Occupational and social impairment** level
- Sleep disturbance, hypervigilance, avoidance behaviors
- Impact on **work and relationships**

> [!important] Be open about suicidal ideation if it applies. The examiner is not there to judge -- they are there to document. Holding back on mental health symptoms is the #1 reason PTSD claims get underrated.

---

**Back/Spine** (`DC 5237/5242`):
- **Range of motion** testing: forward flexion, extension, lateral flexion, rotation
- Pain on **active and passive motion** per `38 CFR 4.59`
- Additional loss during **flare-ups**

**Knees** (`DC 5260/5261`):
- **Flexion and extension** range of motion
- **Stability testing**
- Pain on **weight-bearing and nonweight-bearing**
- Opposite knee tested for **comparison** (required under `38 CFR 4.59`)

**Sleep Apnea** (`DC 6847`):
- Whether you use a **CPAP**
- Frequency of **breathing interruptions**
- **Daytime hypersomnolence**

> [!check] Bring your **sleep study results** to the exam. The examiner needs objective data to confirm severity.

> [!tip] Look up the blank DBQ for your specific condition on VA's website before the exam. It shows you exactly what checkboxes the examiner will fill out -- so you know what to talk about.

sections.heading: :doc: Understanding DBQs (Disability Benefits Questionnaires)
sections.body:
DBQs are the standardized forms examiners fill out during your C&P exam. Each one maps directly to **diagnostic codes and rating criteria** in `38 CFR Part 4`. Understanding your DBQ is like having the answer key before the test.

> [!important] You can find blank DBQ forms on VA's website. Review yours before the exam so you know exactly what will be measured and can prepare your answers accordingly.

---

**For musculoskeletal conditions, the DBQ captures:**
- Range of motion **in degrees**
- **Pain points** during testing
- Functional loss factors under `38 CFR 4.40` and `4.45`: weakness, excess fatigability, incoordination, pain on movement
- **Flare-up impact** -- estimated additional loss
- Additional loss after **repetitive use testing**

**For mental health conditions, the DBQ uses:**
- **Occupational and social impairment levels** that map directly to the General Rating Formula for Mental Disorders
- Checkboxes for **specific symptoms** (intrusive thoughts, nightmares, irritability, etc.)
- An **overall impairment level** assignment

> [!tip] The mental health DBQ impairment levels are the rating tiers: 0%, 10%, 30%, 50%, 70%, 100%. Each tier has specific language about occupational/social impairment. Know which tier matches your actual functioning.

> [!check] Print the blank DBQ for each of your claimed conditions. Go through it line by line and write down what your answer would be. Bring those notes to the exam.

sections.heading: :alert: Common Mistakes to Avoid
sections.body:
These mistakes tank claims every single day. Don't be that veteran.

> [!danger] **Don't say "I'm fine" out of habit.** Don't let pride prevent you from being honest about your limitations. The examiner writes down what you say -- if you say you're fine, that's what goes in the report.

---

**The C&P exam mistake hall of shame:**

| Mistake | What Happens |
|---------|--------------|
| **Skipping the exam** | Almost guarantees a denial |
| **Arguing with the examiner** | They're documenting, not deciding -- stay calm and factual |
| **Exaggerating symptoms** | Examiners detect inconsistencies; undermines ALL your credibility |
| **Taking pain meds before** | Masks your symptoms so the examiner sees a better picture than reality |
| **Assuming they read your file** | Some have, some haven't -- explain your full history regardless |
| **Minimizing out of pride** | Lower documented severity = lower rating = less money |

> [!warning] If you can't make the appointment, call to **reschedule immediately**. A no-show is treated as a failure to report -- and VA can deny your claim based solely on that.

> [!check] Be **truthful but thorough** about your worst days. There's a critical difference between exaggerating and fully describing your actual limitations. The examiner needs the complete picture, not the brave face.

sections.heading: :gavel: Flare-ups and Functional Loss: The DeLuca Factors
sections.body:
This is one of the most powerful tools in your arsenal -- and most veterans have never heard of it.

> [!important] The CAVC case **DeLuca v. Brown** established that VA must consider **functional loss beyond just what shows up on a range-of-motion test**. If you're having a good day at the exam, the DeLuca factors protect you.

---

**The legal framework:**
- `38 CFR 4.40`: Disability includes functional loss from **pain, weakness, and fatigability**
- `38 CFR 4.45`: Examiner must assess **excess fatigability, incoordination, and pain on movement**
- `38 CFR 4.59`: Painful motion with joint pathology is **"productive of disability"** -- actually painful, unstable, or malaligned joints are entitled to **at least the minimum compensable rating**

> [!check] If your condition flares up and you have worse symptoms than what the examiner sees in the office, **say so explicitly**. The examiner is required to ask about flare-ups and estimate additional functional loss during them. If they don't ask, volunteer the information.

**Example of what to say:**

> [!quote] "Today my knee bends to 90 degrees, but during a flare-up -- which happens 3-4 times a month and lasts 2-3 days -- I can barely bend it past 45 degrees and I can't use stairs."

> [!tip] The examiner must document flare-up frequency, duration, and estimated additional range-of-motion loss. If the final report doesn't include this information, the exam is **inadequate** and you can challenge it.

sections.heading: :eye: After the Exam: Review and Respond
sections.body:
The exam is done, but your work isn't. The examiner submits their report to VA within a few days to two weeks. **Get a copy and review it line by line.**

> [!check] Request a copy of your C&P exam report through VA.gov or by calling VA. Do this as soon as possible -- don't wait for the rating decision.

---

**Review the report for these issues:**
- **Factual errors** -- wrong dates, incorrect medical history
- **Missing symptoms** you reported during the exam
- **Inadequate testing** -- no passive motion testing, no flare-up assessment
- **Negative nexus opinion** that contradicts the evidence without explanation
- **Failure to consider** your lay statements and buddy statements

> [!warning] Examiner reports frequently contain errors. Conditions you described in detail may be summarized as "veteran reports mild pain." Dates may be wrong. Testing you know happened may not be documented. Don't assume the report is accurate.

**Your options if the report has problems:**

| Issue | Response |
|-------|----------|
| Factual errors | Submit a **rebuttal statement** pointing out specific inaccuracies |
| Inadequate testing | **Request a new examination** |
| Wrong nexus opinion | Submit a **private DBQ or IMO** from your own provider |

> [!tip] A private DBQ from your own doctor carries the same weight as a VA C&P exam. If the VA examiner got it wrong, your doctor can fill out the same form with the correct findings.

sections.heading: :gavel: When to Request a New Exam
sections.body:
Under `38 CFR 3.159`, VA has a **duty to provide an adequate examination**. If your exam was deficient, you have the right to demand a new one.

> [!important] An inadequate exam is not just annoying -- it's a legal error that can be the basis for overturning a denial on appeal.

---

**An exam is inadequate when:**
- The examiner **didn't review** your claims file or STRs
- Range of motion wasn't tested in **all required planes**
- Pain on **passive motion** wasn't assessed (required by `38 CFR 4.59`)
- The examiner didn't ask about or account for **flare-ups** (required under DeLuca)
- The negative nexus opinion **lacks rationale** beyond a bare conclusion
- The examiner relied on an **inaccurate factual premise**

> [!check] If you received an inadequate exam and your claim is denied, **cite the specific inadequacy in your appeal**. BVA regularly remands cases for new examinations when the original was deficient.

> [!tip] A **Higher-Level Review** (`VA Form 20-0996`) can identify duty-to-assist errors -- including inadequate examinations -- and return the case for correction without you needing to go all the way to the Board.

> [!quote] BVA remands thousands of cases every year specifically because C&P exams were inadequate. Common reasons: no flare-up assessment, no passive range-of-motion testing, and nexus opinions that say "not related" without explaining why.
